items.title: Global Economic Outlook & Strategy: Uncertainties and Resilience
items.summary: Higher oil prices have taken a bite out of the global economy's performance, but the pain has stayed manageable amid continued resilience. We're more sanguine about growth for the rest of the year, as the reality is that the global economy is less dependent on oil than in previous decades.

items.title: Foreign Exchange Forecasts: July 2026
items.summary: We leave our near-term EURUSD forecast unchanged at 1.13 to reflect USD-positive asymmetry around the Fed’s reaction function and the U.S.-Iran conflict. G10 RV trades look increasingly attractive, though, and we forecast upside in EURGBP and NOKSEK, alongside downside in AUDNZD.

items.title: Global Commodities: Wildcards for 2026's Second Half
items.summary: Geopolitical, climate and technological shocks routinely overwhelm traditional supply-demand analysis for commodities, so investors need to consider plausible events that markets are least prepared for. We look at nine potential commodities wildcards for 2026's second half and beyond.

items.title: European Economics: The ECB Lets the Market Do Its Job, for Now
items.summary: While the ECB didn't hike rates, its press conference conveyed a distinct impression that it will do so in September, barring a new and more sustainable ebb in U.S.-Iran tensions. Effectively, it seems oil prices remain the main driver by far of monetary policy for the foreseeable future.

items.title: Alphabet Sees AI Momentum, Capacity Constraints
items.summary: Alphabet’s quarter showed its AI investments are driving results across both Search and Cloud. In this note we look at three debates likely to be front and center after this quarter, all of them fueled by greater AI demand trends across the enterprise and consumer.

items.title: U.S. Engineering & Construction: Second-Quarter Preview
items.summary: We expect E&C second-quarter commentary to remain broadly constructive given healthy end-market trends, but remain selective and prefer companies with the clearest exposure to visible capex cycles and “self-help” potential. PWR and MTZ remain our preferred ideas; we also like J.

items.title: U.S. HealthTech & Distribution: Second-Quarter Preview
items.summary: HealthTech names we cover have staged an impressive recovery following first-quarter results. For the current quarter, we see the most upside from PRVA and TDOC, as discussed herein. We're more cautious on AGL and EVH, as we think the run-up in these stocks leaves little room for upside.

items.title: Global Diversified Metals & Mining: First-Half Previews
items.summary: We expect results from BHP, RIO and S32 to reflect stronger metal prices, lifting earnings. Our cumulative EBITDA estimates for these names is expected to deliver a 26% year-over-year increase for the first half of 2026, with bulk of the increase coming from copper and aluminum exposure.

items.title: Japan Strategy: Looking for a Tech Stock Bottom
items.summary: We see Japan's recent tech stock correction as triggered by a combination of factors, which we explore in this note. But we also see limited additional downside going forward, and think Japan's tech sector will likely outperform the market again toward year's end.

items.title: Global Aerospace & Defense: Takes From the Farnborough Air Show
items.summary: We offer 12 top takes from the Farnborough Air Show, ranging from what we gleaned from aftermarket channel checks and M&A trends to how the aerospace supply chain will be tested and our read on aerospace suppliers’ ability to price.

items.title: Emerging Markets Strategy Weekly: There's Only One Game in Town
items.summary: The carry trade has prevailed in emerging markets so far this year, driven by long LatAm and high-yielding CEEMEA funded out of Asia. It's a strategy that's proven resilient; looking ahead, we assess several catalysts that could challenge the persistence of this dynamic.

items.title: General Motors Looks Set to Accelerate
items.summary: General Motors' results beat expectations and it raised 2026 guidance. GM’s ability to generate an EBIT margin from North American auto operations of 8% to 10% and free cash flow of $10 billion in 2026, despite ongoing tariff and BEV cash payments, are positives supporting our Buy rating.

items.title: Nike Opts for a Big Change in Its China Distribution Strategy
items.summary: Nike has informed Topsports and Pou Sheng, its two largest distribution partners in China, that it will terminate most of its online distribution at year's end. Our initial view is that this is an extreme move and a risky strategy by NKE, for reasons that we explore in this note.

items.title: Global Commodities: A Look at Seasonal Volatility
items.summary: Volatility premia, the difference between implied and subsequent realized vol, are generally negative for corn, wheat and oilseeds from July to October, which coincides with critical stages in the new-crop cycle. Overall, we remain bullish on most agricultural commodities, coffee excepted.

items.title: Oil Monitor: Will We See More Strategic Stocks Released?
items.summary: The IEA/OECD release of 400 million barrels of oil and products is almost done, having increased supply and reduced fear in the market. As discussed herein, we'd expect another material release by September should Strait of Hormuz flows or energy infrastructure be materially compromised.

items.title: UK Economics: Good Cop, Bad Cop?
items.summary: Andy Burnham is now UK Prime Minister, with John Healey his new Chancellor of the Exchequer. We present some fiscal options the government may consider and provide some rough calculations on how much it could cost the new government to raise the personal allowance on income-tax thresholds.

items.title: Africa Weekly: A New Informal Pound Band for Egypt?
items.summary: We consider the second-half economic outlook for Egypt, with a particular focus on whether the Central Bank of Egypt will try to establish a new informal band of the Egyptian pound, as we think was the case after the March 2024 devaluation.

items.title: Equity Markets Positioning Model: Vulnerabilities Remain
items.summary: The recent technology-led selloff triggered a deterioration in positioning across most major equity markets, with long unwinds, new shorts and rising unrealized losses driving flows. Upcoming earnings will be key in determining whether positioning stabilizes or unwinds further.

items.title: We Initiate Coverage of AerCap Holdings at Buy
items.summary: We initiate coverage of AerCap Holdings at Buy. AER is the global leader in aviation leasing and is exposed to the same aerospace megatrends driving many names we cover, but has a unique scale and history of dealmaking as well as an unusually cheap valuation that's never made sense to us.

items.title: EM Equity Strategy Compass: The Broadening Question
items.summary: Despite recent volatility, MSCI EM is up c20% YTD, though gains remain narrow. Broadening in 2H26 depends on cyclical improvement in macro data and EPS revisions, and a pause in Tech outperformance. We upgrade China to Overweight and neutralize Korea while Staying Overweight Taiwan.

items.title: U.S. Equity Strategy: Framing the Growth Narrative
items.summary: This note refreshes our work on “clusters” as a better framework for large-cap positioning. The Growth cluster now represents 55% of S&P 500 market cap and ~48% of earnings. Growth valuations are lower versus history than those of Cyclicals and Defensives.

items.title: Global Semiconductors: Kimi K3, Another Jevon's Paradox
items.summary: Moonshot AI launched Kimi K3, the world’s largest open-weight model with 2.8T parameters. We expect Kimi K3 to drive memory demand for Server DDR5 and eSSD through growth in KV cache demand and AI-agent token volumes. We maintain a constructive view on the memory market.

items.title: Artificial Intelligence: Citi’s Inference Ahead — Open Reaches the Frontier
items.summary: Open-weight models continue to narrow the gap with proprietary models, raising concerns over AI Labs’ economics as the cost of intelligence declines. Higher costs will continue to encourage efforts at routing to more efficient models, driving enterprise adoption and infrastructure needs.

items.title: U.S. Economics Weekly: The Inflation Heatwave Is Breaking
items.summary: Core CPI slowed to 2.6% year over year and is on track to reach 2.3% or below in coming months. The economy is not overheating or generating broad-based inflation. Cooler jobs and inflation data make rate hikes unlikely, and we continue to expect the Fed’s next move to be a rate cut.

items.title: PULSE Monitor: Pick Your Topic
items.summary: Markets had no shortage of focal points this week. Solid bank earnings support our expectation for a normal positive-surprise season, but the bigger story is the Semi/Tech pullback fueling broadening. Be wary of style/factor shifts. Iran and $80 WTI were offset by dovish inflation data.

items.title: Global Macro Strategy: Macro Mezze for the Summer BBQ
items.summary: The summer has delivered several major headlines, from the U.S.-Iran conflict and potential Japan policy changes to AI bubble worries. We look at recent major market developments and adjust our portfolio accordingly. as detailed in this note.

items.title: A Look at 800VDC Architecture for Next-Generation AI Data Centers
items.summary: In this Super-Sector Analysis, we outline the likely implications of the 800VDC transition and potential Asia beneficiaries in the gray space and IT rack power/cooling in AI data centers. We see this as a multi-layer investment opportunity across six segments, discussed herein.

items.title: European Banks: Banking on Markets — Summer 2026
items.summary: It was a blow-out quarter for equity capital markets, especially in the U.S. and Asia, and the backdrop is likely to remain supportive into 2027. The downside for European banks is that U.S. peers are reinvesting into the business, with competitive pressures set to intensify.

items.title: European Auto Manufacturers, Parts & Equipment: Earnings Preview
items.summary: SXAP again finds itself at the bottom of the pack for EU sectors. A lot feels priced in already, but we believe OEM FY26 guidance remains at risk given concerns about EU pricing and China demand. To the extent that guides can be reiterated, we see room for select short squeezes on results.

items.title: General Electric: We've Seen This Movie Before
items.summary: General Electric's EPS results topped consensus and it raised guidance across the board, but the stock reacted negatively, with feedback focused on the fact that the results were priced in. We've seen this movie before; we recommend buying on weakness and reiterate our Buy.

items.title: TSMC Benefits As AI Demand Keeps Accelerating
items.summary: TSMC delivered another strong quarter with revenue of $40.2 billion and third-quarter guidance implying another ~12% sequential increase. More importantly, as discussed in this note, management repeatedly emphasized that AI demand has become even stronger than previously expected.

items.title: Putting Worries Aside, ABT Posts a Very Good Quarter
items.summary: There were plenty of worries heading into Abbott's second quarter, including the state of the medical-device market and patient volumes. But ABT delivered a better-than-expected quarter and may be one of the few stocks with accelerating revenue growth exiting 2026 and into 2027.

items.title: U.S. Broadcasting: We're Moving Fox to Our Top Pick
items.summary: We believe weakness in Fox following the Roku announcement is overdone. We expect ~$1 billion in annual EBITDA from both revenue and cost synergies, suggesting the deal may be free-cash-flow accretive one year after closing. We move Fox to our Top Pick and maintain our $78 target price.

items.title: We're Downgrading Svenska Handelsbanken to Sell
items.summary: There's a lot to like about Handelsbanken, but growth is a challenge, and we're no longer confident of seeing significant growth in Sweden or Norway. We therefore lower our volume growth assumptions and downgrade SHB to Sell. It remains our least-preferred Nordic/European bank.

items.title: European Rates Weekly: Waiting for the Second Round
items.summary: Ahead of the ECB meeting, we discuss several strategic points of interest to investors.
